tech.headline: New cooling panel uses PV and waste cooking oil
tech.summary: Researchers have designed an innovative home cooling panel system. The design incorporates PV power generation, thermoelectric cooling, and latent heat storage using waste cooking oil. Through optimized modeling, results suggest that a single panel can deliver approximately 6–15 kWh/day of cooling, providing a potential new method for residential temperature management using renewable energy.

tech.headline: Chile sets bidding terms for first energy auction including storage
tech.summary: Chile’s CNE has released preliminary terms for the 2026/01 power supply tender. This technology-neutral process favors renewables by excluding high-emission fuels and allowing storage-backed bids. Utilizing 15-year contracts and time-of-day demand blocks, the structure is designed to support photovoltaic and hybrid solar-plus-storage projects, specifically targeting daytime and evening peak coverage requirements.

tech.headline: SNEC 2026 shows solar shifting toward system integration and AI
tech.summary: SNEC 2026 highlighted a significant industry shift from module-centric growth toward system integration. Storage and AI are becoming increasingly central to project design and value creation. At the technology level, back-contact technology gained momentum as a premium platform, while emerging applications and scenario-specific modules signaled a trend toward deeper market segmentation within the solar sector.

tech.headline: Longi develops 27.27% efficient heterojunction back-contact solar cell
tech.summary: Longi and Yangzhou University researchers developed a laser-based manufacturing strategy for HJT back-contact solar cells. This method mitigates shock-wave-induced damage, improving passivation stability and device performance. The approach resulted in a certified 27.27% efficient solar cell, providing an industrially viable pathway toward the fabrication of ultra-high-efficiency photovoltaic technology through improved laser processing techniques.

learn.title: Perovskite Solar Cells: Beyond Silicon Limits
learn.intro: Perovskite solar cells represent a revolutionary leap in photovoltaic technology, offering potentially higher efficiencies than traditional silicon at lower manufacturing costs. As we desperately need better solar tech to meet climate goals, understanding this next-generation material is crucial for the energy transition.
learn.c1_text: Perovskites are crystalline materials with a specific crystal structure named after mineral perovskite (CaTiO3). Think of them like LEGO blocks that can be stacked in different arrangements to capture sunlight more effectively than silicon. They're solution-processable, meaning they can be printed like newspapers rather than requiring expensive high-temperature furnaces.
learn.c2_text: Perovskite cells have achieved over 31% efficiency in laboratory settings, significantly surpassing silicon's typical 20-22% range. The material absorbs light across broader spectrum wavelengths and can be tuned like a dial by adjusting its chemical composition, making it incredibly versatile for capturing more solar energy.
learn.c3_text: India's Department of Science & Technology has allocated ₹50 crore for perovskite research through its Solar Mission initiatives. The Indian Institute of Technology (IIT) Bombay and delhi have developed indigenous perovskite-silicon tandem cells achieving 28% efficiency. With India's vast solar potential, this technology could accelerate the country's 500 GW renewable target by 2030.
learn.stat1: Perovskite cells achieved 31% efficiency in lab tests (2024)
learn.stat2: Silicon solar panels typically reach only 20-22% efficiency
learn.stat3: India invested ₹50 crore in perovskite research (2023-24)
learn.stat4: IIT Bombay developed perovskite-silicon tandem cells at 28% efficiency

learn.title: Second Life EV Batteries: Powering the Grid
learn.intro: Electric vehicle batteries don't become waste after their automotive life - they can store 70-80% of their original capacity and power homes and grids for another decade. This circular economy approach is vital for sustainable energy storage as EV sales explode globally.
learn.c1_text: When EV batteries degrade to 70-80% of their original capacity after 5-10 years in vehicles, they're perfect for stationary storage applications where weight and space are less critical. Think of it like using a slightly aging smartphone battery to power your home - still excellent capacity, just not for your pocket anymore.
learn.c2_text: Second-life batteries cost 30-50% less than new lithium-ion batteries while providing comparable performance for grid storage. A typical 60 kWh EV battery module can provide 4-6 hours of backup power for an average Indian household, making them ideal for solar-plus-storage microgrids.
learn.c3_text: India's Faster Adoption and Manufacturing of Hybrid & Electric Vehicles (FAME) scheme promotes second-life battery usage with subsidies covering 20% of conversion costs. Tata Power and MG Motor India have partnered to deploy 1,000 second-life battery systems for grid stabilization. India aims to recycle 50,000 tons of EV batteries annually by 2030 under its E-Waste (Management) Rules 2022.
learn.stat1: EV batteries retain 70-80% capacity after automotive life
learn.stat2: Second-life batteries cost 30-50% less than new ones
learn.stat3: India's FAME scheme covers 20% of conversion costs
learn.stat4: Tata Power deployed 1,000 second-life battery systems

learn.title: HVDC: Superhighway for Electricity
learn.intro: High Voltage Direct Current (HVDC) transmission lines act like superhighways for electricity, sending power across thousands of kilometers with minimal losses. As India builds the world's largest solar park in Gujarat, HVDC becomes essential for moving renewable energy from deserts to cities efficiently.
learn.c1_text: Unlike alternating current (AC) that loses 5-8% power per 1000 km, HVDC loses only 2-3% over the same distance. Think of it like a perfectly pressurized water pipeline versus one with leaks - HVDC maintains voltage integrity across vast distances. It's like having a dedicated freight train for electricity instead of a regular passenger train.
learn.c2_text: HVDC uses converter stations to change DC to AC at destination, enabling precise control of power flow. Underground and submarine cables handle HVDC better than AC, which is why it connects offshore wind farms to mainland grids. Modern voltage-sourced converters (VSC) allow black-start capabilities, helping restore power during grid failures.
learn.c3_text: India's ±800 kV HVDC transmission system spans 15,000 km, carrying 70% of the nation's power. The 800 km Longyangtzhe-Damao line in China inspired India's 1,400 km ±800 kV Raigarh-Pugalur scheme. India's Green Energy Corridor Phase-I & II allocates ₹40,000 crore for HVDC infrastructure to wheel 40 GW of renewable power by 2025, supporting the country's 500 GW renewable target.
learn.stat1: HVDC loses only 2-3% power per 1000 km vs AC's 5-8%
learn.stat2: India's HVDC network spans 15,000 km carrying 70% of power
learn.stat3: Green Energy Corridor allocates ₹40,000 crore for HVDC
learn.stat4: India plans to wheel 40 GW renewable power via HVDC by 2025

learn.title: Geothermal Energy: Tapping Earth's Core
learn.intro: Geothermal energy harnesses heat from Earth's core to generate clean electricity and provide heating, offering baseload power that runs 24/7 regardless of weather. With climate change demanding reliable renewable sources, geothermal provides the steady backbone our grids desperately need.
learn.c1_text: Deep wells access magma-heated rock formations where water turns to steam, spinning turbines just like fossil fuel plants. Imagine tapping into a perpetual underground hot spring - this heat has been cooking Earth's interior for billions of years and provides consistent energy day and night, unaffected by seasons or weather patterns.
learn.c2_text: Enhanced geothermal injection pumps water into dry rock at high pressure, creating artificial reservoirs where none existed naturally. This technology can unlock geothermal potential almost anywhere, similar to fracking but for energy. Modern plants achieve capacity factors of 90-95%, far exceeding solar's 25% or wind's 35%.
learn.c3_text: India's geothermal map reveals massive potential along the Western Ghats and Himalayan regions, with the 1.3 GW Western Disturbance zone alone capable of powering 3 million homes. The Ministry of New and Renewable Energy plans 500 MW geothermal installation by 2030, focusing on Tamil Nadu's Puglisherai basin where pilot projects show 15 MW potential. India's first commercial geothermal plant at Ambikapur, Chhattisgarh, generates 2 MW continuously, proving the technology's viability.
learn.stat1: Geothermal plants achieve 90-95% capacity factors vs solar's 25%
learn.stat3: Western Ghats offer 1.3 GW Western Disturbance zone
learn.stat4: India's first geothermal plant generates 2 MW continuously

learn.title: Solar Panel Types: Choosing the Best
learn.intro: Not all solar panels are equal - monocrystalline, polycrystalline, and thin-film technologies each offer unique advantages for different applications. Understanding these differences helps optimize rooftop installations and large-scale solar parks for maximum energy generation.
learn.c1_text: Monocrystalline panels use single-crystal silicon slices, appearing blue and uniform with 20-22% efficiency. Polycrystalline panels use fragmented silicon chunks, looking speckled with 15-17% efficiency. Think of it like comparing a perfect diamond (mono) versus a mosaic made of smaller stones (poly) - both precious, but different clarity and performance.
learn.c2_text: Thin-film panels layer photovoltaic material just microns thick onto glass or flexible substrates, enabling curved surfaces and building-integrated applications. Cadmium telluride (CdTe) and amorphous silicon technologies offer 10-11% efficiency but excel in hot climates where traditional panels lose 10-20% performance due to heat.
learn.c3_text: India's National Solar Mission favors polycrystalline panels for its 100 GW ground-mounted projects due to 15% lower cost per watt despite slightly lower efficiency. Tamil Nadu's 500 MW floating solar plant at Mettapadu uses mono-crystalline for higher efficiency in water-splashed environments. The state's 2024 policy mandates 30% local content in panel manufacturing, boosting domestic production of all three technologies under Production Linked Incentive (PLI) schemes worth ₹19,500 crore.
learn.stat2: Polycrystalline panels cost 15% less but offer 15-17% efficiency
learn.stat3: India's PLI scheme allocates ₹19,500 crore for solar manufacturing
learn.stat4: Tamil Nadu's Mettapadu plant uses mono-crystalline on floating structures

learn.title: Green Steel: Hydrogen's Industrial Role
learn.intro: Green steel production replaces coal with hydrogen in blast furnaces, eliminating 1.8 tons of CO2 per ton of steel - critical since steelmaking accounts for 7-9% of global emissions. This breakthrough technology is essential for achieving net-zero targets while meeting growing infrastructure demands.
learn.c1_text: Direct Reduced Iron (DRI) processes substitute coal-derived hydrogen with green hydrogen from water electrolysis, reducing carbon emissions by 95%. Picture a steel mill where instead of burning coal, we're blowing pure hydrogen through iron ore pellets - the hydrogen burns cleanly, leaving behind metallic iron ready for rolling into steel.
learn.c2_text: Green hydrogen costs $3-5/kg today but projected to drop to $1-2/kg by 2030 with renewable scaling. Swedish company HYBRIT produced the world's first fossil-free steel in 2021, while India's Tata Steel aims for 20% hydrogen substitution by 2025. Electric arc furnaces using recycled scrap steel already produce low-carbon steel, complementing green hydrogen DRI pathways.
learn.c3_text: India's National Hydrogen Mission allocates ₹10,000 crore for green hydrogen steel projects, targeting 30 million tons of green steel annually by 2030. Reliance Industries plans India's first 2 million ton green steel plant in Jammu & Kashmir using solar-powered electrolysis. The Steel Authority of India (SAIL) targets 25% hydrogen blending in blast furnaces by 2027, potentially reducing 15 million tons of CO2 emissions yearly - equivalent to removing 3 million cars from roads.
learn.stat1: Green steel eliminates 1.8 tons CO2 per ton of steel produced
learn.stat2: Global steel production accounts for 7-9% of emissions
learn.stat3: India targets 30 million tons green steel by 2030
learn.stat4: SAIL aims for 25% hydrogen blending reducing 15 million tons CO2
